event: Hunter-gatherer Pygmy peoples inhabit the equatorial rainforests of the Congo Basin, among the region's earliest known inhabitants.
Bantu-speaking peoples begin migrating into the region, gradually introducing agriculture, ironworking, and settled village life.
Bantu expansion intensifies, displacing or absorbing earlier populations and establishing the linguistic foundations of the region.
Iron-working Bantu communities along the Congo River and coast develop trade networks and chiefdoms.
The Kingdom of Loango emerges along the Atlantic coast north of the Congo River, becoming a major regional power.
The Kingdom of Kongo and the Tio (Teke) Kingdom flourish, controlling trade in copper, ivory, and cloth across the region.
Portuguese explorer Diogo Cao reaches the mouth of the Congo River, initiating European contact with the coastal kingdoms.
Loango grows wealthy through trade with Europeans, exporting copper, ivory, and increasingly enslaved people.
The transatlantic slave trade expands dramatically, with the coast around Loango Bay becoming a major slaving port.
Coastal kingdoms decline as the Atlantic slave trade peaks and then begins to face abolitionist pressure.
French-Italian explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza begins expeditions into the region for France.
Brazza signs a treaty with Makoko, the Teke king, placing the territory under French protection and founding what becomes Brazzaville.
The Berlin Conference formalizes the partition of Africa, recognizing French claims north of the Congo River.
France formally establishes the colony of French Congo over the territory.
The colony is reorganized as Middle Congo (Moyen-Congo) under French administration.
Middle Congo becomes part of the federation of French Equatorial Africa, with Brazzaville as its capital.
Construction begins on the Congo-Ocean Railway, whose brutal forced-labor conditions cause tens of thousands of African deaths.
The Congo-Ocean Railway linking Brazzaville to the port of Pointe-Noire is completed.
Charles de Gaulle convenes the Brazzaville Conference, outlining post-war reforms for France's African colonies.
Middle Congo gains representation in the French Union and a territorial assembly under the French Fourth Republic.
The territory votes to become an autonomous republic within the French Community, taking the name Republic of the Congo.
The Republic of the Congo gains full independence from France on 15 August, with Fulbert Youlou as first president.
The newly independent nation is admitted to the United Nations on 20 September.
Mass protests known as Les Trois Glorieuses force President Youlou to resign, bringing Alphonse Massamba-Debat to power.
Captain Marien Ngouabi seizes power in a coup, ousting Massamba-Debat.
Ngouabi proclaims the People's Republic of the Congo, Africa's first Marxist-Leninist state, and founds the ruling PCT party.
President Marien Ngouabi is assassinated, and Joachim Yhombi-Opango assumes power.
Denis Sassou Nguesso becomes president, beginning a long era of dominance over Congolese politics.
Facing economic crisis and democratic pressure, the PCT abandons Marxism-Leninism and adopts multiparty politics.
A national conference strips President Sassou Nguesso of most powers and restores the country's name to Republic of the Congo.
Pascal Lissouba wins the country's first multiparty democratic presidential election.
Disputed legislative elections trigger violent clashes between rival political militias in Brazzaville.
A civil war erupts; backed by Angolan forces, Sassou Nguesso ousts President Lissouba and returns to power.
A ceasefire and peace accords begin to wind down the devastating civil war that displaced hundreds of thousands.
A new constitution is approved and Sassou Nguesso wins a presidential election; renewed fighting flares in the Pool region.
A peace agreement is signed with Ninja rebels led by Pastor Frederic Bintsamou (Ntumi) in the Pool region.
Sassou Nguesso is re-elected president amid an opposition boycott and disputes over the vote.
A controversial referendum removes presidential term and age limits, allowing Sassou Nguesso to seek another term amid deadly protests.
Sassou Nguesso wins a disputed presidential election, sparking renewed armed conflict in the Pool region.
A ceasefire is reached with Pool-region militias after fighting displaces over 100,000 people.
Facing heavy debt amid low oil prices, the Congo secures an IMF bailout program.
Sassou Nguesso wins re-election as his main rival, Guy-Brice Parfait Kolelas, dies of COVID-19 on election day.
The Republic of the Congo hosts the Three Basins Summit in Brazzaville to coordinate protection of the world's tropical rainforests.
